title: How Do I Ask a Professional for an Informational Interview?
author: Tklesson Team
content:
Your professor just assigned a career exploration project. You need to interview someone working in your dream field. The problem is, you don't know anyone in that field. You're going to have to send a cold email or a LinkedIn message to a complete stranger, and the thought makes your stomach drop. What do you say? How do you get them to respond? Here is how to craft a request that professionals actually want to say yes to.

Why People Say Yes

Most professionals remember being in your position. Someone helped them when they were starting out, and many are genuinely willing to pay that forward. An informational interview is a low-stakes commitment. You're asking for twenty minutes of their time, not a job. You're asking for advice, not a favor. That distinction matters.

People also like talking about themselves and their careers. It's flattering to be recognized as someone worth learning from. Your request, when framed correctly, is not an imposition. It's a compliment.

The Template That Works

Here is a cold email template you can adapt. It works because it's specific, respectful of their time, and makes responding easy.

Subject: Quick question from a [Your University] student interested in [Their Field]

Dear [Name],

My name is [Your Name] and I'm a [year] at [University] studying [major]. I've been following your work in [specific thing they do] and I'm really interested in learning more about how you got started in [their field].

I know your schedule is busy, but would you be open to a 15-minute phone call or video chat in the next few weeks? I'm working on a career exploration project for class, and I'd love to hear about your path.

Thanks for considering it. I'm happy to work around your schedule.

Best, [Your Name]

Why This Template Works

It tells them who you are immediately. It shows you've actually looked at their work, which proves this isn't a mass email. It asks for a specific, small amount of time. It gives them a reason you're reaching out, the class project, which makes the request feel legitimate and low-pressure. And it ends by deferring to their schedule, which makes saying yes easy.

Where to Find People to Ask

LinkedIn is your best tool. Search for alumni from your university who work in your target field. Alumni are dramatically more likely to respond than strangers with no shared connection. Your university's career center may also have an alumni mentorship database. Use it.

Look for people who are three to ten years into their careers. Senior executives are harder to reach and often have less time. Someone who graduated five years ago remembers exactly what you're going through and is more likely to make time for you.

Professional associations often have member directories. Industry conferences and events list speakers on their websites. These are all fair game. The worst thing that can happen is they don't respond. The best thing that can happen is they say yes and become a valuable contact in your network.

If They Don't Respond

Send one follow-up message after a week. Keep it brief and gracious. "Hi [Name], I wanted to follow up on my previous message in case it got buried. No pressure at all, just wanted to make sure you saw it. Thanks again for considering."

If they don't respond to the follow-up, let it go. People get busy. Emails get missed. It's not personal. Move on to the next person on your list.

During the Interview

Come prepared with questions. Do not ask things you could have Googled. Ask about their day-to-day work, what surprised them about the field, what skills they use most, and what they wish they had known when they were in your position. Take notes. Be present. End the conversation by thanking them and asking if there's anyone else they'd recommend you speak with. This is how one informational interview becomes a network.

The Bottom Line

Cold contacting a professional feels intimidating, but it's a skill that pays off for your entire career. Use a specific, respectful template. Reach out to alumni and people a few years ahead of you. Prepare thoughtful questions. Most people want to help. You just have to make it easy for them to say yes.
